# .NET AI and Machine Learning

## Inputs

| Input | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| Task description | Yes | What the AI/ML feature should accomplish (e.g., "classify support tickets", "summarize documents") |
| Data description | Yes | Type and shape of input data (structured/tabular, unstructured text, images, mixed) |
| Deployment constraints | No | Cloud vs. local, latency SLO, cost budget, offline requirements |
| Existing project context | No | Current .csproj, existing packages, target framework |

## Workflow

### Step 1: Classify the task using the decision tree

Evaluate the developer's task against this decision tree and select the appropriate technology. State which branch applies and why.

| Task type | Technology | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Structured/tabular data: classification, regression, clustering, anomaly detection, recommendation | **ML.NET** (`Microsoft.ML`) | Reproducible (given a fixed seed and dataset), no cloud dependency, purpose-built models for these tasks |
| Natural language understanding, generation, summarization, reasoning over unstructured text (single prompt → response, no tool calling) | **LLM via Microsoft.Extensions.AI** (`IChatClient`) | Requires language model capabilities beyond pattern matching; no orchestration needed |
| Agentic workflows: tool/function calling, multi-step reasoning, agent loops, multi-agent collaboration | **Microsoft Agent Framework** (`Microsoft.Agents.AI`) built on top of **Microsoft.Extensions.AI** | Requires orchestration, tool dispatch, iteration control, and guardrails that `IChatClient` alone does not provide |
| Building GitHub Copilot extensions, custom agents, or developer workflow tools | **GitHub Copilot SDK** (`GitHub.Copilot.SDK`) | Integrates with the Copilot agent runtime for IDE and CLI extensibility |
| Running a pre-trained or fine-tuned custom model in production | **ONNX Runtime** (`Microsoft.ML.OnnxRuntime`) | Hardware-accelerated inference, model-format agnostic |
| Local/offline LLM inference with no cloud dependency | **OllamaSharp** with local [AI models supported by Ollama](https://ollama.com/search) | Privacy-sensitive, air-gapped, or cost-constrained scenarios |
| Semantic search, RAG, or embedding storage | **Microsoft.Extensions.VectorData.Abstractions** + a vector database provider (e.g., Azure AI Search, Milvus, MongoDB, pgvector, Pinecone, Qdrant, Redis, SQL) | Provider-agnostic abstractions for vector similarity search; pair with a database-specific connector package (many are moving to community toolkits) |
| Ingesting, chunking, and loading documents into a vector store | **Microsoft.Extensions.AI.DataIngestion** (preview) + **Microsoft.Extensions.VectorData.Abstractions** (MEVD) | Handles document parsing, text chunking, embedding generation, and upserting into a vector database; pairs with Microsoft.Extensions.VectorData.Abstractions |
| Both structured ML predictions AND natural language reasoning | **Hybrid**: ML.NET for predictions + LLM for reasoning layer | Keep loosely coupled; ML.NET handles reproducible scoring, LLM adds explanation |

**Critical rule:** Do NOT use an LLM for tasks that ML.NET handles well (classification on tabular data, regression, clustering). LLMs are slower, more expensive, and non-deterministic for these tasks.

### Step 1b: Select the correct library layer

After identifying the task type, select the right library layer. These libraries form a stack — each builds on the one below it. Using the wrong layer is a major source of non-deterministic agent behavior.

| Layer | Library | NuGet package | Use when |
|-------|---------|---------------|----------|
| **Abstraction** | Microsoft.Extensions.AI (MEAI) | `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` | You need a provider-agnostic interface for chat, embeddings, or tool calling. This is the foundation — always include it. Use `IChatClient` directly **only** for simple prompt-in/response-out scenarios with no tool calling or agentic loops. If the task involves tools, agents, or multi-step reasoning, you must add the Orchestration layer above. |
| **Provider SDK** | OpenAI, Azure.AI.OpenAI, Azure.AI.Inference, OllamaSharp | `OpenAI`, `Azure.AI.OpenAI`, `Azure.AI.Inference`, `OllamaSharp` | You need a concrete LLM provider implementation. These wire into MEAI via `AddChatClient`. Use `OpenAI` for direct OpenAI access, `Azure.AI.OpenAI` for Azure OpenAI, `Azure.AI.Inference` for Azure AI Foundry / GitHub Models, or `OllamaSharp` for local Ollama. Use directly only if you need provider-specific features not exposed through MEAI. |
| **Orchestration** | Microsoft Agent Framework | `Microsoft.Agents.AI` (prerelease) | The task involves tool/function calling, agentic loops, multi-step reasoning, multi-agent coordination, durable context, or graph-based workflows. **This is required whenever the scenario involves agents or tools — do not hand-roll tool dispatch loops with `IChatClient`.** Builds on top of MEAI. **Note:** This package is currently prerelease — use `dotnet add package Microsoft.Agents.AI --prerelease` to install it. |
| **Copilot integration** | GitHub Copilot SDK | `GitHub.Copilot.SDK` | You are building extensions or tools that integrate with the GitHub Copilot runtime — custom agents, IDE extensions, or developer workflow automation that leverages the Copilot agent platform. |

#### Decision rules for library selection

1. **Start with MEAI.** Every AI integration begins with `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` for the `IChatClient` / `IEmbeddingGenerator` abstractions. This ensures provider-swappability and testability.

2. **Add a provider SDK** (`OpenAI`, `Azure.AI.OpenAI`) as the concrete implementation behind MEAI. Do not call the provider SDK directly in business logic — always go through the MEAI abstraction.

3. **Use Agent Framework (`Microsoft.Agents.AI`) for any task that involves tools or agents.** If the task is a single prompt → response with no tool calling, MEAI is sufficient. **You MUST use `Microsoft.Agents.AI`** when any of these apply:
   - Tool/function calling (agent decides which tools to invoke)
   - Multi-step reasoning with state carried across turns
   - Agentic loops that iterate until a goal is met
   - Multi-agent collaboration with handoff protocols
   - Graph-based or durable workflows

   Do **not** implement these patterns by hand with `IChatClient` — the Agent Framework provides iteration limits, observability, and tool dispatch that are error-prone to reimplement.

4. **Add Copilot SDK only when building Copilot extensions.** Use `GitHub.Copilot.SDK` when the goal is to build a custom agent or tool that runs inside the GitHub Copilot platform (CLI, IDE, or Copilot Chat). This is not a general-purpose LLM orchestration library — it is specifically for Copilot extensibility.

5. **Never skip layers.** Do not use Agent Framework without MEAI underneath. Do not call `HttpClient` to OpenAI alongside MEAI in the same workflow. Each layer depends on the one below it.

### Step 2: Select packages and set up the project

Install only the packages needed for the selected technology branch. Do not mix competing abstractions.

#### Classic ML packages

```xml
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.ML" Version="4.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.ML.AutoML" Version="0.*" />
<!-- Only if custom numerical work is needed: -->
PackageReference Include="System.Numerics.Tensors" Version="10.*"
<PackageReference Include="MathNet.Numerics" Version="5.*" />
<!-- Only for data exploration: -->
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Data.Analysis" Version="0.*" />
```

> **Do NOT use** Accord.NET — it is archived and unmaintained.

#### Modern AI packages

```xml
<!-- Always start with the abstraction layer -->
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI" Version="9.*" />

<!-- Orchestration (agents, workflows, tools, memory) — prerelease; use dotnet add package Microsoft.Agents.AI --prerelease -->
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Agents.AI" Version="1.*-*" />

<!-- Cloud LLM provider (pick one) -->
<PackageReference Include="Azure.AI.OpenAI" Version="2.*" />
<!-- OR -->
<PackageReference Include="OpenAI" Version="2.*" />

<!-- Client-side token counting for cost management -->
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.ML.Tokenizers" Version="2.*" 

<!-- Local LLM inference -->
<PackageReference Include="OllamaSharp" Version="5.*" />

<!-- Custom model inference -->
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.ML.OnnxRuntime" Version="1.*" />

<!-- Vector store abstraction -->
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.VectorData.Abstractions" Version="9.*" />

<!-- Document ingestion, chunking, and vector store loading (preview) -->
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI.DataIngestion" Version="9.*-*" />

<!-- Copilot platform extensibility -->
<PackageReference Include="GitHub.Copilot.SDK" Version="1.*" />
```

> **Stack coherence rule:** Never mix raw SDK calls (`HttpClient` to OpenAI) with `Microsoft.Extensions.AI`, Microsoft Agent Framework, or Copilot SDK in the same workflow. Pick one abstraction layer per workflow boundary and commit to it. See Step 1b for the layering rules.

#### Register services with dependency injection

All AI/ML services must be registered via DI. Never instantiate clients directly in business logic.

```csharp
// Configuration via IOptions<T>
services.Configure<AiOptions>(configuration.GetSection("AI"));

// Register the AI client through the abstraction
services.AddChatClient(builder => builder
    .UseOpenAIChatClient("gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"));
```

### Step 3: Implement with guardrails

Apply the guardrails for the selected technology branch. Every generated implementation must follow these rules.

#### Classic ML guardrails

1. **Reproducibility**: Always set a random seed in the ML context:
   ```csharp
   var mlContext = new MLContext(seed: 42);
   ```

2. **Data splitting**: Always split into train/test (and optionally validation). Never evaluate on training data:
   ```csharp
   var split = mlContext.Data.TrainTestSplit(data, testFraction: 0.2);
   ```

3. **Metrics logging**: Always compute and log evaluation metrics appropriate to the task:
   ```csharp
   var metrics = mlContext.BinaryClassification.Evaluate(predictions);
   logger.LogInformation("AUC: {Auc:F4}, F1: {F1:F4}", metrics.AreaUnderRocCurve, metrics.F1Score);
   ```

4. **AutoML first**: Prefer `mlContext.Auto()` for initial model selection, then refine manually.

5. **PredictionEngine pooling**: In ASP.NET Core, always use the pooled prediction engine — never a singleton:
   ```csharp
   services.AddPredictionEnginePool<ModelInput, ModelOutput>()
       .FromFile(modelPath);
   ```

#### LLM integration guardrails

1. **Temperature**: Always set explicitly. Use `0` for factual/deterministic tasks:
   ```csharp
   var options = new ChatOptions
   {
       Temperature = 0f,
       MaxOutputTokens = 1024,
   };
   ```

2. **Structured output**: Always parse LLM output into strongly-typed objects with fallback handling:
   ```csharp
   var result = await chatClient.GetResponseAsync<MySchema>(prompt, options, cancellationToken);
   ```

3. **Retry logic**: Always implement retry with exponential backoff:
   ```csharp
   services.AddChatClient(builder => builder
       .UseOpenAIChatClient(modelId)
       .Use(new RetryingChatClient(maxRetries: 3)));
   ```

4. **Cost control**: Always estimate and log token usage. Use Microsoft.ML.Tokenizers to count tokens client-side before sending requests so you can enforce budgets proactively. Choose the smallest model tier that meets quality requirements (e.g., gpt-4o-mini before gpt-4o).

5. **Secret management**: Never hardcode API keys. Use Azure Key Vault, user-secrets, or environment variables:
   ```csharp
   var apiKey = configuration["AI:ApiKey"]
       ?? throw new InvalidOperationException("AI:ApiKey not configured");
   ```

6. **Model version pinning**: Specify exact model versions to reduce behavioral drift:
   ```csharp
   // Pin to a specific dated version, not just "gpt-4o"
   var modelId = "gpt-4o-2024-08-06";
   ```

#### Agentic workflow guardrails

0. **Use `Microsoft.Agents.AI` for all agentic workflows.** Do not implement tool dispatch loops or multi-step agent reasoning by hand with `IChatClient`. The Agent Framework provides `ChatClientAgent` (or `AgentWorker`) which handles the tool call → result → re-prompt cycle with built-in guardrails. All rules below assume you are using `Microsoft.Agents.AI`.

1. **Iteration limits**: Always cap agentic loops to prevent runaway execution:
   ```csharp
   var settings = new AgentInvokeOptions
   {
       MaximumIterations = 10,
   };
   ```

2. **Cost ceiling**: Implement a token budget per execution and terminate when reached. Use Microsoft.ML.Tokenizers to count prompt and completion tokens locally and compare against the budget before each iteration.

3. **Observability**: Log non-sensitive metadata for every agent step. Never log raw `message.Content` — it may contain user prompts, tool outputs, secrets, or PII that persist in plaintext in central logging systems:
   ```csharp
   await foreach (var message in agent.InvokeStreamingAsync(history, settings))
   {
       logger.LogDebug("Agent step: Role={Role}, ContentLength={Length}",
           message.Role, message.Content?.Length ?? 0);
   }
   ```

4. **Tool schemas**: Define explicit tool/function schemas with descriptions. Never rely on implicit tool discovery.

5. **Simplicity preference**: Prefer single-agent with tools over multi-agent unless the task genuinely requires agent collaboration.

#### RAG guardrails

1. **Embedding caching**: Never re-embed the same content on every query. Cache embeddings in the vector store.

2. **Chunking strategy**: Use semantic chunking (split on paragraph/section boundaries) over fixed-size chunking. Ensure chunks have enough context to be useful on their own.

3. **Relevance thresholds**: Do not inject low-relevance chunks into context. Set a minimum similarity score:
   ```csharp
   var results = await vectorStore.SearchAsync(query, new VectorSearchOptions
   {
       Top = 5,
       MinimumScore = 0.75f,
   });
   ```

4. **Source attribution**: Track which chunks contributed to the final response. Include source references in the output.

5. **Batch embeddings**: Batch embedding API calls where possible to reduce latency and cost.

### Step 4: Handle non-determinism

When the solution involves LLM calls or agentic workflows, explicitly address non-determinism:

1. **Acknowledge it**: Inform the developer that LLM outputs are non-deterministic even at temperature 0 (due to batching, quantization, and model updates).

2. **Validate outputs**: Implement schema validation and content assertion checks on every LLM response.

3. **Graceful degradation**: Design a fallback path for when the LLM returns unexpected, malformed, or empty output:
   ```csharp
   var response = await chatClient.GetResponseAsync<ClassificationResult>(prompt, options);
   if (response is null || !response.IsValid())
   {
       logger.LogWarning("LLM returned invalid response, falling back to rule-based classifier");
       return ruleBasedClassifier.Classify(input);
   }
   ```

4. **Evaluation harness**: For any prompt that will be iterated on, recommend creating a golden dataset and evaluation scaffold to measure prompt quality over time.

5. **Model version pinning**: Pin to specific dated model versions (e.g., `gpt-4o-2024-08-06`) to reduce drift between deployments.

### Step 5: Apply performance and cost controls

1. **Connection pooling**: Use `IHttpClientFactory` and DI-managed clients for all external services.

2. **Response caching**: Cache repeated or similar queries. Consider semantic caching for LLM responses where appropriate.

3. **Streaming**: Use `IAsyncEnumerable` for LLM responses in user-facing scenarios to reduce time-to-first-token:
   ```csharp
   await foreach (var update in chatClient.GetStreamingResponseAsync(prompt, options))
   {
       yield return update.Text;
   }
   ```

4. **Health checks**: Implement health checks for external AI service dependencies:
   ```csharp
   services.AddHealthChecks()
       .AddCheck<OpenAIHealthCheck>("openai");
   ```

5. **ML.NET prediction pooling**: In web applications, always use `PredictionEnginePool<TIn, TOut>`, never a single `PredictionEngine` instance (it is not thread-safe).

### Step 6: Validate the implementation

1. Build the project and verify no warnings:
   ```bash
   dotnet build -c Release -warnaserror
   ```

2. Run tests, including integration tests that validate AI/ML behavior:
   ```bash
   dotnet test -c Release
   ```

3. For ML.NET pipelines, verify that evaluation metrics meet the project's quality bar and that the model can be serialized and loaded correctly.

4. For LLM integrations, verify that structured output parsing handles both valid and malformed responses.

5. For RAG pipelines, verify that retrieval returns relevant results and that irrelevant chunks are filtered out.

## Validation

- [ ] Technology selection follows the decision tree — LLMs are not used for tasks ML.NET handles
- [ ] All AI/ML services are registered via dependency injection
- [ ] Configuration uses `IOptions<T>` pattern — no hardcoded values
- [ ] API keys are loaded from secure sources — not in source code or committed config files
- [ ] ML.NET pipelines set a random seed and split data for evaluation
- [ ] LLM calls set temperature, max tokens, and retry logic explicitly
- [ ] Agentic workflows have iteration limits and cost ceilings
- [ ] RAG pipelines implement chunking, relevance thresholds, and source attribution
- [ ] Non-deterministic outputs have validation and fallback paths
- [ ] `dotnet build -c Release -warnaserror` completes cleanly

## Anti-Patterns to Reject

When reviewing or generating code, flag and redirect the developer if any of these patterns are detected:

| Anti-pattern | Redirect |
|-------------|----------|
| Using an LLM for classification on structured/tabular data | Use ML.NET instead — it is faster, cheaper, and deterministic |
| Calling LLM APIs without retry or timeout logic | Add `RetryingChatClient` or Polly-based retry with exponential backoff |
| Storing API keys in `appsettings.json` committed to source control | Use user-secrets (dev), environment variables, or Azure Key Vault (prod) |
| Using Accord.NET for new projects | Migrate to ML.NET — Accord.NET is archived and unmaintained |
| Building custom neural networks in .NET from scratch | Use a pre-trained model via ONNX Runtime or call an LLM API |
| RAG without chunking strategy or relevance filtering | Implement semantic chunking and set a minimum similarity score threshold |
| Agentic loops without iteration limits or cost ceilings | Add `MaximumIterations` and a token budget ceiling |
| Using MEAI `IChatClient` with raw `HttpClient` calls to the same provider | Pick one abstraction layer and commit to it |
| Implementing tool calling or agentic loops manually with `IChatClient` instead of using `Microsoft.Agents.AI` | Use `Microsoft.Agents.AI` — it provides iteration limits (`MaximumIterations`), built-in tool dispatch, observability hooks, and cost controls. Hand-rolled loops lack these guardrails. |
| Using Agent Framework for a single prompt→response call | Use MEAI `IChatClient` directly — Agent Framework is for multi-step orchestration |
| Using Copilot SDK for general-purpose LLM apps | Copilot SDK is for Copilot platform extensions only — use MEAI + Agent Framework for standalone apps |
| Calling OpenAI SDK directly in business logic instead of through MEAI | Register the provider via `AddChatClient` and depend on `IChatClient` in business code |
| Using `PredictionEngine` as a singleton in ASP.NET Core | Use `PredictionEnginePool<TIn, TOut>` — `PredictionEngine` is not thread-safe |
| Using `Func<ReadOnlySpan<T>>` for delegates with ref struct parameters | Define a custom delegate type — ref structs cannot be generic type arguments |
| Using `Microsoft.SemanticKernel` for new projects | Use `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` + `Microsoft.Agents.AI` — Semantic Kernel is superseded by these newer abstractions for LLM orchestration and tool calling |

## Common Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Solution |
|---------|----------|
| Over-engineering with LLMs | Start with the simplest approach (rules, ML.NET) and add LLM capability only when simpler methods fall short |
| Evaluating ML models on training data | Always use `TrainTestSplit` and report metrics on the held-out test set |
| LLM output drift between deployments | Pin to specific dated model versions (e.g., `gpt-4o-2024-08-06`) |
| Token cost surprises | Set `MaxOutputTokens`, use Microsoft.ML.Tokenizers for accurate client-side token counting, log token counts per request, and alert on budget thresholds |
| Non-reproducible ML training | Set `MLContext(seed: N)` and version your training data alongside the code |
| RAG returning irrelevant context | Set a minimum similarity score and limit the number of injected chunks |
| Cold start latency on ML.NET models | Pre-warm the `PredictionEnginePool` during application startup |
| Microsoft Agent Framework + raw OpenAI SDK in same class | Choose one orchestration layer per workflow boundary |
data.description: Guides technology selection and implementation of AI and ML features in .NET 8+ applications using ML.NET, Microsoft.Extensions.AI (MEAI), Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF), GitHub Copilot SDK, ONNX Runtime, and OllamaSharp. Covers the full spectrum from classic ML through modern LLM orchestration to local inference. Use when adding classification, regression, clustering, anomaly detection, recommendation, LLM integration (text generation, summarization, reasoning), RAG pipelines with vector search, agentic workflows with tool calling, Copilot extensions, or custom model inference via ONNX Runtime to a .NET project. DO NOT USE FOR projects targeting .NET Framework (requires .NET 8+), the task is pure data engineering or ETL with no ML/AI component, or the project needs a custom deep learning training loop (use Python with PyTorch/TensorFlow, then export to ONNX for .NET inference).

result.content:
# About Microsoft Agent Framework

Microsoft Agent Framework is a comprehensive .NET library for building, orchestrating, and deploying AI agents and multi-agent workflows. The framework provides everything from simple chat agents to complex multi-agent systems with graph-based orchestration capabilities.

## Key Features

- **Multi-Agent Orchestration**: Coordinate multiple agents using sequential, concurrent, group chat, and handoff patterns
- **Graph-based Workflows**: Connect agents and functions with streaming, checkpointing, and human-in-the-loop capabilities, with both imperative or declarative workflow support
- **Multiple Provider Support**: Seamlessly integrate with various LLM providers with more being added continuously
- **Extensible Middleware**: Flexible request/response processing with custom pipelines and exception handling
- **Built-in Observability**: OpenTelemetry integration for distributed tracing, monitoring, and debugging
- **Cross-Platform**: Compatible with .NET 8.0, .NET Standard 2.0, and .NET Framework for broad deployment options

Whether you're building simple AI assistants or complex multi-agent systems, Microsoft Agent Framework provides the tools and abstractions needed to create robust, scalable AI applications in .NET.

# Getting Started ⚡

- Learn more at the [documentation site](https://learn.microsoft.com/agent-framework/overview/agent-framework-overview).
- Join the [Discord community](https://discord.gg/b5zjErwbQM).
- Follow the team on [Semantic Kernel blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/semantic-kernel/).
- Check out the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/microsoft/agent-framework) for the latest updates.

result.content:
# Microsoft.Extensions.AI

.NET developers need to integrate and interact with a growing variety of artificial intelligence (AI) services in their apps. The `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` libraries provide a unified approach for representing generative AI components, and enable seamless integration and interoperability with various AI services.

## The packages

The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions) package provides the core exchange types, including [`IChatClient`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.ichatclient) and [`IEmbeddingGenerator<TInput,TEmbedding>`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.iembeddinggenerator-2). Any .NET library that provides an LLM client can implement the `IChatClient` interface to enable seamless integration with consuming code.

The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI) package has an implicit dependency on the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions` package. This package enables you to easily integrate components such as automatic function tool invocation, telemetry, and caching into your applications using familiar dependency injection and middleware patterns. For example, it provides the [`UseOpenTelemetry(ChatClientBuilder, ILoggerFactory, String, Action<OpenTelemetryChatClient>)`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions.useopentelemetry#microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions-useopentelemetry(microsoft-extensions-ai-chatclientbuilder-microsoft-extensions-logging-iloggerfactory-system-string-system-action((microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclient)))) extension method, which adds OpenTelemetry support to the chat client pipeline.

## Which package to reference

Libraries that provide implementations of the abstractions typically reference only `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`.

To also have access to higher-level utilities for working with generative AI components, reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package instead (which itself references `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`). Most consuming applications and services should reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package along with one or more libraries that provide concrete implementations of the abstractions.

## Install the package

From the command-line:

```console
dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.AI
```

Or directly in the C# project file:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI" Version="[CURRENTVERSION]" />
</ItemGroup>
```

## Documentation

Refer to the [Microsoft.Extensions.AI libraries documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai) for more information and API usage examples.

## Feedback & Contributing

We welcome feedback and contributions in [our GitHub repo](https://github.com/dotnet/extensions).
<exited with exit code 0>

## Which package to reference

Libraries that provide implementations of the abstractions typically reference only `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`.

To also have access to higher-level utilities for working with generative AI components, reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package instead (which itself references `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`). Most consuming applications and services should reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package along with one or more libraries that provide concrete implementations of the abstractions.

## Install the package

From the command-line:

```console
dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.AI
```

Or directly in the C# project file:

```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI" Version="[CURRENTVERSION]" />
</ItemGroup>
```

## Documentation

Refer to the [Microsoft.Extensions.AI libraries documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai) for more information and API usage examples.

## Feedback & Contributing

We welcome feedback and contributions in [our GitHub repo](https://github.com/dotnet/extensions).

result.content: Provides an that delegates to an implementation. Security considerations: The orchestrates data flow across trust boundaries. The underlying AI service is an external endpoint and LLM responses should be treated as untrusted output. Developers should be aware of: Hallucination: LLMs may generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information. Do not treat LLM output as authoritative without verification. Indirect prompt injection: Data retrieved by tools, AI context providers, or chat history providers may contain adversarial content designed to influence LLM behavior or exfiltrate data through tool calls. Malicious payloads: LLM output may contain content that is harmful if rendered or executed without sanitization — for example, HTML/JavaScript for cross-site scripting, SQL for injection, or shell commands. Tool invocation: By default, all tools provided to the agent are invoked without user approval. The AI selects which functions to call and with what arguments. Function arguments should be treated as untrusted input. Developers should require explicit approval for tools with side effects, data sensitivity, or irreversibility. Developers should validate and sanitize LLM output before rendering it in HTML, executing it as code, using it in database queries, or passing it to any security-sensitive context. Apply defense-in-depth by combining tool approval requirements with output validation. Provides extension methods for to enable discoverability of . Initializes a new instance of the class. The chat client to use when running the agent. Optional system instructions that guide the agent's behavior. These instructions are provided to the with each invocation to establish the agent's role and behavior. Optional name for the agent. This name is used for identification and logging purposes. Optional human-readable description of the agent's purpose and capabilities. This description can be useful for documentation and agent discovery scenarios. Optional collection of tools that the agent can invoke during conversations. These tools augment any tools that may be provided to the agent via when the agent is run. By default, all provided tools are invoked without user approval. The AI selects which functions to call and chooses the arguments — these arguments should be treated as untrusted input. Developers should require explicit approval for tools that have side effects, access sensitive data, or perform irreversible operations.

            <summary>
            Represents metadata for a chat client agent, including its identifier, name, instructions, and description.

            <remarks>
            This class is used to encapsulate information about a chat client agent, such as its unique
            identifier, display name, operational instructions, and a descriptive summary. It can be used to store and transfer

            </remarks>

<member name="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions">
            <summary>
            Provides specialized run options for <see cref="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgent"/> instances, extending the base agent run options with chat-specific configuration.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            This class extends <see cref="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentRunOptions"/> to provide additional configuration options that are specific to
            chat client agents, in particular <see cref="P:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions.ChatOptions"/>.
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="M:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions.#ctor(Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions)">
            <summary>
            Initializes a new instance of the <see cref="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions"/> class.
            </summary>
            <param name="chatOptions">
            Optional chat options to customize the behavior of the chat client during this specific agent invocation.
            These options will be merged with the default chat options configured for the agent.
            </param>
        </member>
        <member name="M:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions.#ctor(Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions)">
            <summary>
            Initializes a new instance of the <see cref="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions"/> class by copying values from the specified options.
            </summary>
            <param name="options">The options instance from which to copy values.</param>
        </member>
        <member name="P:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions.ChatOptions">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets the chat options to apply to the agent invocation.
            </summary>
            <value>
            Chat options that control various aspects of the chat client's behavior, such as temperature, max tokens,
            tools, instructions, and other model-specific parameters. If <see langword="null"/>, the agent's default
            chat options will be used.
            </value>
            <remarks>
            These options are specific to this invocation and will be combined with the agent's default chat options.
            If both the agent and this run options specify the same option, the run options value typically takes precedence.
            In the case of collections, like <see cref="P:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions.Tools"/>, the collections will be unioned.
            </remarks>
        </member>
        <member name="P:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions.ChatClientFactory">
            <summary>
            Gets or sets a factory function that can replace (typically via decorators) the chat client on a per-request basis.
            </summary>
            <value>
            A function that receives the agent's configured chat client and returns a potentially modified or entirely
            different chat client to use for this specific invocation. If <see langword="null"/>, the agent's default
            chat client will be used without modification.
            </value>
        </member>
        <member name="M:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentRunOptions.Clone">
            <inheritdoc/>
<exited with exit code 0>

python3 << 'EOF'
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import re

# Parse Microsoft.Agents.AI.xml
agents_xml = "/home/runner/.nuget/packages/microsoft.agents.ai/1.5.0/lib/net9.0/Microsoft.Agents.AI.xml"
tree = ET.parse(agents_xml)
root = tree.getroot()

# Find all ChatClientAgent members
print("=== ChatClientAgent Type Members ===\n")
for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
    name = member.get('name', '')
    if name.startswith('M:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgent'):
        # Extract method name
        method_match = re.search(r'M:Microsoft\.Agents\.AI\.ChatClientAgent\.(.+?)[\(\)]', name)
        if method_match:
            method = method_match.group(1)

import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import re

abstractions_xml = "/home/runner/.nuget/packages/microsoft.extensions.ai.abstractions/10.5.1/lib/net8.0/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions.xml"
tree = ET.parse(abstractions_xml)
root = tree.getroot()

def extract_members(class_name):
    print(f"\n=== {class_name} ===\n")
    for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
        name = member.get('name', '')
        if name.startswith(f'T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.{class_name}'):
            summary = member.find("summary")
            if summary is not None and summary.text:
                text = summary.text.strip()[:300]
                print(f"Type Summary: {text}")
            break
    
    # Find properties and methods
    members = []
    for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
        name = member.get('name', '')
        if f'{class_name}.' in name and (name.startswith('P:') or name.startswith('M:')):
            members.append((name, member))
    
    for name, member in sorted(members)[:15]:
        # Extract member name
        match = re.search(r'(M|P):Microsoft\.Extensions\.AI\.' + class_name + r'\.(.+?)[\(]?', name)
        if match:
            member_type = match.group(1)
            member_name = match.group(2)
            if member_type == 'P':
                print(f"  Property: {member_name}")
            else:
                print(f"  Method: {member_name}")

extract_members("AITool")
extract_members("AIFunction")
extract_members("AIFunctionFactory")
extract_members("ChatMessage")
extract_members("ChatOptions")

EOF

python3 << 'EOF'
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

# Parse Agents.AI XML
agents_xml = "/home/runner/.nuget/packages/microsoft.agents.ai/1.5.0/lib/net9.0/Microsoft.Agents.AI.xml"
tree = ET.parse(agents_xml)
root = tree.getroot()

# Show AgentRunOptions
print("="*60)
print("Type: Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentRunOptions")
print("="*60)

for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
    name = member.get('name', '')
    if name == 'T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentRunOptions':
        summary = member.find("summary")
        if summary is not None and summary.text:
            text = ' '.join(summary.text.split())
            print(f"\nSummary: {text[:400]}\n")
        break

# Find AgentRunOptions members
print("Members:")
for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
    name = member.get('name', '')
    if 'AgentRunOptions.' in name:
        if name.startswith('P:'):
            prop = name.split('.')[-1]
            print(f"  Property: {prop}")
        elif name.startswith('M:'):
            method = name.split('.')[-1]
            if not method.startswith('#'):
                print(f"  Method: {method}")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("Type: Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent")
print("="*60 + "\n")

for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
    name = member.get('name', '')
    if name == 'T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent':
        summary = member.find("summary")
        if summary is not None and summary.text:
            text = ' '.join(summary.text.split())
            print(f"Summary: {text[:500]}\n")
        break

# Find AIAgent members
print("Key Members:")
for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
    name = member.get('name', '')
    if name.startswith('M:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent.'):
        method = name.split('.')[-1]
        if 'Run' in method or 'Stream' in method:
            print(f"  Method: {method}")

EOF

result.content: Provides an that delegates to an implementation. Security considerations: The orchestrates data flow across trust boundaries. The underlying AI service is an external endpoint and LLM responses should be treated as untrusted output. Developers should be aware of: Hallucination: LLMs may generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information. Do not treat LLM output as authoritative without verification. Indirect prompt injection: Data retrieved by tools, AI context providers, or chat history providers may contain adversarial content designed to influence LLM behavior or exfiltrate data through tool calls. Malicious payloads: LLM output may contain content that is harmful if rendered or executed without sanitization — for example, HTML/JavaScript for cross-site scripting, SQL for injection, or shell commands. Tool invocation: By default, all tools provided to the agent are invoked without user approval. The AI selects which functions to call and with what arguments. Function arguments should be treated as untrusted input. Developers should require explicit approval for tools with side effects, data sensitivity, or irreversibility. Developers should validate and sanitize LLM output before rendering it in HTML, executing it as code, using it in database queries, or passing it to any security-sensitive context. Apply defense-in-depth by combining tool approval requirements with output validation. Provides extension methods for to enable discoverability of . Initializes a new instance of the class. The chat client to use when running the agent. Optional system instructions that guide the agent's behavior. These instructions are provided to the with each invocation to establish the agent's role and behavior. Optional name for the agent. This name is used for identification and logging purposes. Optional human-readable description of the agent's purpose and capabilities. This description can be useful for documentation and agent discovery scenarios. Optional collection of tools that the agent can invoke during conversations. These tools augment any tools that may be provided to the agent via when the agent is run. By default, all provided tools are invoked without user approval. The AI selects which functions to call and chooses the arguments — these arguments should be treated as untrusted input. Developers should require explicit approval for tools that have side effects, access sensitive data, or perform irreversible operations. Optional logger factory for creating loggers used by the agent and its components. Optional service provider for resolving dependencies required by AI functions and other agent components. This is particularly important when using custom tools that require dependency injection. This is only relevant when the doesn't already contain a and the needs to insert one. is . Initializes a new instance of the class. The chat client to use when running the agent. Configuration options that control all aspects of the agent's behavior, including chat settings, chat history provider factories, context provider factories, and other advanced configurations. import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
from textwrap import fill

def extract_full_type_doc(xml_file, type_name, full_typename):
    """Extract complete documentation for a type"""
    tree = ET.parse(xml_file)
    root = tree.getroot()
    
    print(f"\n{'='*70}")
    print(f"{type_name}: {full_typename}")
    print('='*70)
    
    # Find type definition
    for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
        name = member.get('name', '')
        if name == full_typename:
            summary = member.find("summary")
            if summary is not None and summary.text:
                text = ' '.join(summary.text.split())
                print(f"\nSummary:\n{fill(text, width=65)}\n")
            break
    
    # Find constructors
    constructors = []
    for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
        name = member.get('name', '')
        if '#ctor' in name and type_name in name:
            constructors.append((name, member))
    
    if constructors:
        print("CONSTRUCTORS:")
        for name, member in constructors[:3]:  # Show first 3 constructors
            # Extract params
            params = member.findall("param")
            if params:
                print(f"  Constructor:")
                for p in params:
                    pname = p.get('name', '')
                    ptext = p.text or ''
                    ptext = ' '.join(ptext.split())[:100]
                    print(f"    - {pname}: {ptext}...")
            else:
                print(f"  Constructor (no parameters)")
    
    # Find properties
    properties = []
    for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
        name = member.get('name', '')
        if name.startswith(f'P:{full_typename.split(":")[1]}.'):
            properties.append((name, member))
    
    if properties:
        print("\nPROPERTIES:")
        for name, member in properties[:10]:
            # Extract property name
            prop_name = name.split('.')[-1]
            summary = member.find("summary")
            if summary is not None and summary.text:
                text = ' '.join(summary.text.split())[:60]
                print(f"  {prop_name}: {text}")
            else:
                print(f"  {prop_name}")
    
    # Find key methods
    methods = []
    for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
        name = member.get('name', '')
        base_name = full_typename.split(":")[-1]
        if f'M:{full_typename.split(":")[1]}.{base_name}.' in name and '#' not in name:
            methods.append((name, member))
    
    if methods:
        print("\nMETHODS:")
        for name, member in methods[:8]:
            # Extract method name
            method_part = name.split('.')[-1].split('(')[0]
            print(f"  {method_part}")

import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import re

agents_file = "/home/runner/.nuget/packages/microsoft.agents.ai/1.5.0/lib/net9.0/Microsoft.Agents.AI.xml"
tree = ET.parse(agents_file)
root = tree.getroot()

print("="*70)
print("ChatClientAgent.RunAsync Methods")
print("="*70 + "\n")

# Find RunAsync methods
run_methods = []
for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
    name = member.get('name', '')
    if 'ChatClientAgent.RunAsync' in name or 'ChatClientAgent.RunStreamingAsync' in name:
        # Extract method signature
        match = re.search(r'M:Microsoft\.Agents\.AI\.ChatClientAgent\.(RunAsync[A-Za-z`]*)\((.+?)\)', name)
        if match:
            method_name = match.group(1)
            params_str = match.group(2)
            
            # Clean up parameter string
            # Parse param types
            param_types = []
            if params_str:
                parts = params_str.split(',')
                for part in parts:
                    # Extract just the type name
                    type_match = re.search(r'(?:System\.)?(?:Collections\.Generic\.)?(\w+(?:`\d)?(?:\[\])?)', part)
                    if type_match:
                        param_types.append(type_match.group(1))
            
            run_methods.append((method_name, param_types))

# Remove duplicates and display
seen = set()
for method_name, param_types in sorted(run_methods):
    key = (method_name, tuple(param_types))
    if key not in seen:
        seen.add(key)
        params = ", ".join(param_types)
        print(f"{method_name}({params})")

print("\n" + "="*70)
print("ChatClientAgentOptions - Configuration")
print("="*70 + "\n")

# Find ChatClientAgentOptions properties
for member in root.findall(".//member[@name]"):
    name = member.get('name', '')
    if name.startswith('P:Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentOptions.'):
        prop_name = name.split('.')[-1]
        summary = member.find("summary")
        if summary is not None and summary.text:
            text = ' '.join(summary.text.split())[:80]
            print(f"  {prop_name}: {text}")

EOF

print("\nChatRole ENUM VALUES:")

print("  - Tool: Represents tool result/feedback")

print("\n6. AITool - Tool Specification for LLM")

print("  - string Description { get; }")
print("  - Dictionary<string, object> AdditionalProperties { get; }")

print("\n7. AIFunction - Executable Tool Implementation")

print("  - Task<object> InvokeCoreAsync(AIFunctionArguments arguments, CancellationToken ct)")

print("\n8. AIFunctionFactory - Tool Creation")

print("")
print("  - static AIFunction Create(MethodInfo method, object instance = null, string name = null, string description = null)")
print("")
print("  - static AIFunctionDeclaration CreateDeclaration(AIFunction function)")

print("\nUSAGE EXAMPLE:")

print('      (string query) => SearchDatabase(query),')
print('      name: "search",')
print('      description: "Search the database"')
print("  );")

print("\n9. ChatOptions - Request Configuration")
print("-" * 80)
print("LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions")

print("\nCONSTRUCTORS:")
print("  - ChatOptions()")
print("  - ChatOptions(ChatOptions options)  // Copy constructor")

print("\nKEY PROPERTIES FOR TOOL CALLING:")
print("  - string ModelId { get; set; }: Which model to use")
print("  - double Temperature { get; set; }: Creativity (0-2)")
print("  - int MaxOutputTokens { get; set; }: Response length limit")
print("  - string Instructions { get; set; }: System prompt")
print("  - ChatToolMode ToolMode { get; set; }: How to handle tools (Auto, None, Required, etc.)")
print("  - IList<AITool> Tools { get; set; }: Available tools for this request")
print("  - string ConversationId { get; set; }: Track multi-turn conversations")

print("\n10. FunctionInvokingChatClient - Automatic Tool Execution")
print("-" * 80)
print("LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient")
print("DESCRIPTION: Middleware that automatically invokes tool calls from LLM responses")

print("\nUSAGE:")
print("  var chatClient = new OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient(...)")
print("      .AsBuilder()")
print("      .UseFunctionInvoking()")
print("      .Build();")
print("")
print("  // chatClient now automatically invokes functions when LLM requests them")

print("\n11. ChatToolMode - Tool Invocation Control")
print("-" * 80)
print("LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatToolMode")
print("DESCRIPTION: Enum controlling how the LLM can use tools")

print("\nVALUES:")
print("  - Auto: LLM decides whether to use tools")
print("  - None: Tools disabled")
print("  - Required: LLM must use a tool")
print("  - Specific tools can be required via RequiredChatToolMode")

print("\n" + "="*80)
print("COMPLETE TOOL CALLING FLOW")
print("="*80)

print("""
1. CREATE TOOLS using AIFunctionFactory:
   var tool = AIFunctionFactory.Create(
       (string query) => database.Search(query),
       name: "search",
       description: "Search database"
   );

2. CONFIGURE AGENT with tools:
   var agent = new ChatClientAgent(
       chatClient,
       instructions: "You are a helpful assistant",
       tools: new[] { tool }  // Can add multiple tools
   );

3. CREATE SESSION:
   var session = await agent.CreateSessionAsync();

4. ADD INITIAL MESSAGE:
   session.AddMessage(ChatRole.User, "Search for 'foo'");

5. RUN AGENT:
   var options = new ChatClientAgentRunOptions();
   var response = await agent.RunAsync(session, options);

6. TOOL EXECUTION IS AUTOMATIC:
   - ChatClientAgent uses FunctionInvokingChatClient internally
   - LLM response triggers tool calls automatically
   - Tool results are added to conversation
   - Agent continues (agentic loop)

7. GET FINAL RESULT:
   foreach (var msg in session.Messages)
   {
       if (msg.Role == ChatRole.Assistant)
           Console.WriteLine(msg.Text);
   }
""")

5. ChatMessage and ChatRole - Message Types

  - Role { get; set; }

  - MessageId { get; set; }
  - AdditionalProperties { get; set; }

ChatRole ENUM VALUES:
  - User: Represents user input
  - Assistant: Represents agent/LLM response
  - System: Represents system-level instructions
  - Tool: Represents tool result/feedback

6. AITool - Tool Specification for LLM
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AITool
DESCRIPTION: Represents a tool that can be called by the LLM

AITool PROPERTIES:
  - string Name { get; }
  - string Description { get; }
  - Dictionary<string, object> AdditionalProperties { get; }

7. AIFunction - Executable Tool Implementation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunction
DESCRIPTION: Represents a function that can be invoked and described to the LLM

AIFunction PROPERTIES:
  - MethodInfo UnderlyingMethod { get; }
  - JsonSerializerOptions JsonSerializerOptions { get; }

AIFunction KEY METHODS:
  - Task<object> InvokeAsync(AIFunctionArguments arguments, CancellationToken ct)
  - Task<object> InvokeCoreAsync(AIFunctionArguments arguments, CancellationToken ct)

8. AIFunctionFactory - Tool Creation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunctionFactory
DESCRIPTION: Factory for creating AIFunction instances from delegates/methods

KEY STATIC METHODS:
  - static AIFunction Create<TDelegate>(TDelegate function, string name = null, string description = null)
    where TDelegate : Delegate

  - static AIFunction Create(Delegate function, string name = null, string description = null)

  - static AIFunction Create(MethodInfo method, object instance = null, string name = null, string description = null)

  - static AIFunctionDeclaration CreateDeclaration(AIFunction function)

USAGE EXAMPLE:
  var tool = AIFunctionFactory.Create(
      (string query) => SearchDatabase(query),
      name: "search",
      description: "Search the database"
  );

9. ChatOptions - Request Configuration
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions

CONSTRUCTORS:
  - ChatOptions()
  - ChatOptions(ChatOptions options)  // Copy constructor

KEY PROPERTIES FOR TOOL CALLING:
  - string ModelId { get; set; }: Which model to use
  - double Temperature { get; set; }: Creativity (0-2)
  - int MaxOutputTokens { get; set; }: Response length limit
  - string Instructions { get; set; }: System prompt
  - ChatToolMode ToolMode { get; set; }: How to handle tools (Auto, None, Required, etc.)
  - IList<AITool> Tools { get; set; }: Available tools for this request
  - string ConversationId { get; set; }: Track multi-turn conversations

10. FunctionInvokingChatClient - Automatic Tool Execution
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient
DESCRIPTION: Middleware that automatically invokes tool calls from LLM responses

USAGE:
  var chatClient = new OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient(...)
      .AsBuilder()
      .UseFunctionInvoking()
      .Build();

  // chatClient now automatically invokes functions when LLM requests them

11. ChatToolMode - Tool Invocation Control
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LOCATION: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatToolMode

  - Required: LLM must use a tool

   var tool = AIFunctionFactory.Create(

   var agent = new ChatClientAgent(

   var session = await agent.CreateSessionAsync();

4. ADD INITIAL MESSAGE:
   session.AddMessage(ChatRole.User, "Search for 'foo'");

5. RUN AGENT:
   var options = new ChatClientAgentRunOptions();
   var response = await agent.RunAsync(session, options);

6. TOOL EXECUTION IS AUTOMATIC:

7. GET FINAL RESULT:
   foreach (var msg in session.Messages)

       if (msg.Role == ChatRole.Assistant)
           Console.WriteLine(msg.Text);

arguments.command:
python3 << 'EOF'
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

print("""
================================================================================
COMPLETE API REFERENCE: MICROSOFT.AGENTS.AI & MICROSOFT.EXTENSIONS.AI
Building Agents with Tool Calling
================================================================================

PART 1: CORE TYPES FROM MICROSOFT.AGENTS.AI
================================================================================

TYPE 1: ChatClientAgent
Location: Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgent
Base Class: AIAgent

FULL CONSTRUCTORS:
  
  public ChatClientAgent(
      IChatClient chatClient,
      string instructions = null,
      string name = null,
      string description = null,
      IList<AITool> tools = null,
      ILoggerFactory loggerFactory = null,
      IServiceProvider services = null)
  
  Parameters:
    - chatClient: The IChatClient implementation to use (required)
    - instructions: System-level instructions guiding agent behavior
    - name: Display name for identification/logging
    - description: Human-readable description of capabilities
    - tools: Initial set of AITool instances available to agent
    - loggerFactory: Optional logger factory for diagnostics
    - services: Service provider for dependency injection in tools
  
  Throws: ArgumentNullException if chatClient is null
  
  ------
  
  public ChatClientAgent(
      IChatClient chatClient,
      ChatClientAgentOptions options,
      ILoggerFactory loggerFactory = null,
      IServiceProvider services = null)
  
  Parameters:
    - chatClient: The IChatClient implementation (required)
    - options: Full ChatClientAgentOptions configuration
    - loggerFactory: Optional logger factory
    - services: Optional service provider

PROPERTIES:
  
  public IChatClient ChatClient { get; }
    - Access the underlying chat client
  
  public IChatHistoryProvider ChatHistoryProvider { get; }
    - Get the configured history provider
  
  public IList<AIContextProvider> AIContextProviders { get; }
    - Get list of context providers
  
  public string Name { get; }
  public string Description { get; }
  public string Instructions { get; }
  public ChatOptions ChatOptions { get; }

RUN METHODS (return AgentResponse):
  
  // String input variants
  public Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
      string userMessage,
      AgentSession session,
      ChatClientAgentRunOptions options = null,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)
  
  // ChatMessage input
  public Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
      ChatMessage message,
      AgentSession session,
      ChatClientAgentRunOptions options = null,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)
  
  // Multiple messages
  public Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
      IEnumerable<ChatMessage> messages,
      AgentSession session,
      ChatClientAgentRunOptions options = null,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)
  
  // Session-only (uses existing messages)
  public Task<AgentResponse> RunAsync(
      AgentSession session,
      ChatClientAgentRunOptions options = null,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)

STREAMING RUN METHODS (return IAsyncEnumerable<AgentResponseUpdate>):
  
  public IAsyncEnumerable<AgentResponseUpdate> RunStreamingAsync(
      string userMessage,
      AgentSession session,
      ChatClientAgentRunOptions options = null,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)
  
  // Similar variants for ChatMessage, IEnumerable<ChatMessage>, session-only

GENERIC RUN METHODS (for JSON deserialization):
  
  public Task<T> RunAsync<T>(
      string userMessage,
      AgentSession session,
      JsonSerializerOptions jsonOptions,
      ChatClientAgentRunOptions options = null,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)
  
  // Similar variants for other input types

---

TYPE 2: ChatClientAgentOptions
Location: Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgentOptions

DESCRIPTION: Configuration object for creating ChatClientAgent instances

PROPERTIES:
  
  public string Id { get; set; }
    - Unique agent identifier
  
  public string Name { get; set; }
    - Display name for the agent

TYPE 14: FunctionInvokingChatClient
Location: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient
Implements: IChatClient

DESCRIPTION: Middleware that auto-invokes tool calls

BEHAVIOR:
  - Wraps an IChatClient
  - Intercepts responses with FunctionCallContent
  - Automatically invokes the referenced AIFunction
  - Adds tool result back to conversation
  - Re-invokes chat client for LLM to process result
  - Continues until no more tool calls (agentic loop)

CONFIGURATION:
  - Specifies which tools are invocable
  - Can require approval for tool execution
  - Configurable loop limits

USAGE:
  1. Created automatically by ChatClientAgent
  2. Can be used standalone:
     
     var chatClient = new OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient(apiKey, "gpt-4");
     var tools = new[] { tool1, tool2 };
     
     var wrappedClient = new FunctionInvokingChatClient(
         chatClient,
         tools: tools
     );
     
     var response = await wrappedClient.CompleteAsync(messages);

================================================================================
PART 4: TYPE RELATIONSHIPS & DATA FLOW
================================================================================

INITIALIZATION FLOW:
  
  1. Create IChatClient
     var chatClient = new OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient(apiKey, "gpt-4");
  
  2. Create Tools
     var tool = AIFunctionFactory.Create(
         (string q) => Search(q),
         name: "search"
     );
  
  3. Create ChatClientAgent
     var agent = new ChatClientAgent(
         chatClient,
         instructions: "You are helpful",
         tools: new[] { tool }
     );

EXECUTION FLOW (per call to RunAsync):
  
  1. User calls: agent.RunAsync(userMessage, session)
  
  2. Agent creates AgentSession if needed
  
  3. Agent creates ChatOptions with:
     - Default options from agent
     - Runtime options from ChatClientAgentRunOptions
     - Tools combined
  
  4. Agent retrieves chat history from ChatHistoryProvider
  
  5. Agent calls ChatHistory providers for context
  
  6. ChatMessage list built from:
     - System instructions
     - Context messages
     - Chat history
     - New user message
  
  7. FunctionInvokingChatClient wraps calls:
     a. Send messages to LLM
     b. LLM may request tool calls
     c. Tools automatically invoked
     d. Tool results added to conversation
     e. Repeat until LLM responds with text
  
  8. Final response processed
  
  9. ChatHistoryProvider stores conversation
  
  10. AgentResponse returned with final message

---

TOOL INVOCATION DETAILS:

When LLM decides to call a tool:
  
  1. LLM returns FunctionCallContent in response
     - Includes tool name and parameters (JSON)
  
  2. FunctionInvokingChatClient:
     - Finds matching AITool by name
     - Casts to AIFunction
     - Deserializes parameters to AIFunctionArguments
     - Calls InvokeAsync(arguments)
  
  3. AIFunction:
     - Deserializes JSON parameters
     - Invokes underlying MethodInfo
     - Serializes return value
     - Returns Task<object>
  
  4. Tool result wrapped as ToolContent
  
  5. Added to ChatMessage with Role=Tool
  
  6. Agent loops: send updated conversation back to LLM

================================================================================
KEY CONCEPTS SUMMARY
================================================================================

AGENT LOOP ITERATION:
  
  1. Messages In   -> [system, context, history, user query]
  2. LLM Call      -> Send to ChatOptions.ToolMode=Auto
  3. LLM Response  -> May include tool calls
  4. Auto Execute  -> FunctionInvokingChatClient handles
  5. Update History-> Add tool results
  6. Check Loop    -> If more tool calls needed, go to step 2
  7. Return Result -> When LLM responds with text

TOOLS AS FIRST-CLASS CITIZENS:
  
  - AITool: Specification (name, description, schema)
  - AIFunction: Implementation (executable code)
  - AIFunctionFactory: Creates AIFunction from .NET methods
  - Automatic discovery: Parameters/return types from reflection
  - JSON serialization: Automatic marshalling of parameters
  - Type safety: Compile-time method signatures

CONFIGURATION HIERARCHY:
  
  Agent-Level (ChatClientAgentOptions):
    - Applied to all runs
    - Default instructions, tools, history provider
  
  Run-Level (ChatClientAgentRunOptions):
    - Per-call overrides
    - Can inject per-request ChatClient decorator
    - ChatOptions override/merge
  
  Request-Level (ChatOptions):
    - Temperature, max tokens, model
    - Tools list (union with agent tools)
    - Instructions (supplement agent instructions)

TOOL CONTROL:
  
  ChatOptions.ToolMode controls LLM behavior:
    - Auto: Use tools when beneficial
    - None: Can't use tools
    - Required: Must use a tool
  
  Function approval workflows:
    - ToolApprovalAgent can require human review
    - Tool result can be intercepted/modified
    - Reject tool execution if needed

================================================================================
SECURITY CONSIDERATIONS (from docs)
================================================================================

1. HALLUCINATION
   - LLM may invent facts
   - Validate outputs from tool results

2. INDIRECT PROMPT INJECTION
   - Tool results might contain adversarial content
   - Sanitize data before passing to tools

3. MALICIOUS PAYLOADS
   - LLM output can contain harmful content
   - Sanitize before rendering HTML/executing

4. TOOL INVOCATION
   - By default, all tools invoked without approval
   - Consider ToolApprovalAgent for sensitive operations
   - Treat function arguments as untrusted input

RECOMMENDATIONS:
  - Validate and sanitize all LLM output
  - Apply defense-in-depth (multiple layers)
  - Require approval for sensitive tool operations
  - Use tool execution policies
  - Implement audit logging

================================================================================
""")

    - Returns function result or null
  
  public Task<object> InvokeCoreAsync(
      AIFunctionArguments arguments,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)
    - Core invocation (called by InvokeAsync)
  
  public AIFunctionDeclaration AsDeclarationOnly()
    - Get declaration without execution capability

---

TYPE 11: AIFunctionFactory
Location: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunctionFactory

DESCRIPTION: Factory for creating AIFunction instances from .NET methods/delegates

STATIC METHODS:
  
  public static AIFunction Create<TDelegate>(
      TDelegate function,
      string name = null,
      string description = null)
    where TDelegate : Delegate
    
    Creates AIFunction from a delegate
    Parameters auto-discovered from delegate signature
    Return type auto-discovered
    
    Example:
      var searchTool = AIFunctionFactory.Create(
          (string query) => database.Search(query),
          name: "search_database",
          description: "Search products in database"
      );
  
  ------
  
  public static AIFunction Create(
      Delegate function,
      string name = null,
      string description = null)
    
    Creates from untyped Delegate
  
  ------
  
  public static AIFunction Create(
      MethodInfo method,
      object instance = null,
      string name = null,
      string description = null)
    
    Creates from MethodInfo
    instance: for instance methods, null for static
    
    Example:
      var tool = AIFunctionFactory.Create(
          typeof(MyClass).GetMethod("MyMethod"),
          instance: myInstance,
          name: "my_tool"
      );
  
  ------
  
  public static AIFunctionDeclaration CreateDeclaration(AIFunction function)
    
    Get JSON schema representation for the LLM
    Without execution capability

---

TYPE 12: AIFunctionArguments
Location: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunctionArguments

DESCRIPTION: Passes arguments to AIFunction.InvokeAsync()

USAGE:
  - Keyed collection of parameter names -> values
  - Auto-marshalled to target function's parameters
  - JSON serializable

---

TYPE 13: ChatToolMode & Related
Location: Microsoft.Extensions.AI

ENUM: ChatToolMode
  
  Auto (or null)
    - LLM decides whether/when to use tools
  
  None
    - Tools disabled for this request
  
  Required
    - LLM must invoke a tool (use tool call instead of text)
  
Special modes:
  - AutoChatToolMode
  - NoneChatToolMode  
  - RequiredChatToolMode

---

TYPE 14: FunctionInvokingChatClient
Location: Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient
Implements: IChatClient

DESCRIPTION: Middleware that auto-invokes tool calls

BEHAVIOR:
  - Wraps an IChatClient
  - Intercepts responses with FunctionCallContent
  - Automatically invokes the referenced AIFunction
  - Adds tool result back to conversation
  - Re-invokes chat client for LLM to process result
  - Continues until no more tool calls (agentic loop)

CONFIGURATION:
  - Specifies which tools are invocable
  - Can require approval for tool execution
  - Configurable loop limits

USAGE:
  1. Created automatically by ChatClientAgent
  2. Can be used standalone:
     
     var chatClient = new OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient(apiKey, "gpt-4");
     var tools = new[] { tool1, tool2 };
     
     var wrappedClient = new FunctionInvokingChatClient(
         chatClient,
         tools: tools
     );
     
     var response = await wrappedClient.CompleteAsync(messages);

================================================================================
PART 3: TYPES FROM MICROSOFT.AGENTS.AI.ABSTRACTIONS
================================================================================

TYPE 15: AIAgent (Base Class)
Location: Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent

DESCRIPTION: Abstract base for all agent implementations

ABSTRACT METHODS:
  
  protected abstract Task<AgentResponse> RunCoreAsync(
      IEnumerable<ChatMessage> messages,
      AgentSession session,
      AgentRunOptions options,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken)
    
    - Implement in derived classes
    - ChatClientAgent provides implementation
  
  protected abstract IAsyncEnumerable<AgentResponseUpdate> RunCoreStreamingAsync(
      IEnumerable<ChatMessage> messages,
      AgentSession session,
      AgentRunOptions options,
      CancellationToken cancellationToken)
    
    - Streaming variant

PUBLIC RUN METHODS (convenience overloads):
  - RunAsync(string message, ...)
  - RunAsync(ChatMessage message, ...)
  - RunAsync(IEnumerable<ChatMessage> messages, ...)
  - RunStreamingAsync(...) [same variants]

---

TYPE 16: AgentRunOptions (Base)
Location: Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentRunOptions

DESCRIPTION: Base class for run options

PROPERTIES:
  - Used by derived classes (ChatClientAgentRunOptions)
  - Can store agent-specific settings

---

TYPE 17: AIContextProvider
Location: Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIContextProvider

DESCRIPTION: Injects dynamic context before each run

PURPOSE:
  - Retrieve relevant data based on current conversation
  - Add context as special messages or instructions
  - Examples: user profile, search results, database queries

USAGE:
  // Implement AIContextProvider
  class UserProfileProvider : AIContextProvider
  {
      public async Task<IEnumerable<ChatMessage>> GetContextAsync(...)
      {
          // Retrieve user profile
          // Return as context messages
      }
  }
  
  // Register with agent
  options.AIContextProviders.Add(new UserProfileProvider());

---

TYPE 18: IChatHistoryProvider
Location: Microsoft.Agents.AI.IChatHistoryProvider

DESCRIPTION: Custom history retrieval and persistence

METHODS:
  - RetrieveHistoryAsync()
  - PersistHistoryAsync()

PURPOSE:
  - Store/load conversations from database
  - Multi-session support
  - History compaction/summarization

================================================================================
PART 4: TYPE RELATIONSHIPS & DATA FLOW
================================================================================

INITIALIZATION FLOW:
  
  1. Create IChatClient
     var chatClient = new OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient(apiKey, "gpt-4");
  
  2. Create Tools
     var tool = AIFunctionFactory.Create(
         (string q) => Search(q),
         name: "search"
     );
  
  3. Create ChatClientAgent
     var agent = new ChatClientAgent(
         chatClient,
         instructions: "You are helpful",
         tools: new[] { tool }
     );

EXECUTION FLOW (per call to RunAsync):
  
  1. User calls: agent.RunAsync(userMessage, session)
  
  2. Agent creates AgentSession if needed
  
  3. Agent creates ChatOptions with:
     - Default options from agent
     - Runtime options from ChatClientAgentRunOptions
     - Tools combined
  
  4. Agent retrieves chat history from ChatHistoryProvider
  
  5. Agent calls ChatHistory providers for context
  
  6. ChatMessage list built from:
     - System instructions
     - Context messages
     - Chat history
     - New user message
  
  7. FunctionInvokingChatClient wraps calls:
     a. Send messages to LLM
     b. LLM may request tool calls
     c. Tools automatically invoked
     d. Tool results added to conversation
     e. Repeat until LLM responds with text
  
  8. Final response processed
  
  9. ChatHistoryProvider stores conversation
  
  10. AgentResponse returned with final message

---

TOOL INVOCATION DETAILS:

When LLM decides to call a tool:
  
  1. LLM returns FunctionCallContent in response
     - Includes tool name and parameters (JSON)
  
  2. FunctionInvokingChatClient:
     - Finds matching AITool by name
     - Casts to AIFunction
     - Deserializes parameters to AIFunctionArguments
     - Calls InvokeAsync(arguments)
  
  3. AIFunction:
     - Deserializes JSON parameters
     - Invokes underlying MethodInfo
     - Serializes return value
     - Returns Task<object>
  
  4. Tool result wrapped as ToolContent
  
  5. Added to ChatMessage with Role=Tool
  
  6. Agent loops: send updated conversation back to LLM

================================================================================
KEY CONCEPTS SUMMARY
================================================================================

AGENT LOOP ITERATION:
  
  1. Messages In   -> [system, context, history, user query]
  2. LLM Call      -> Send to ChatOptions.ToolMode=Auto
  3. LLM Response  -> May include tool calls
  4. Auto Execute  -> FunctionInvokingChatClient handles
  5. Update History-> Add tool results
  6. Check Loop    -> If more tool calls needed, go to step 2
  7. Return Result -> When LLM responds with text

TOOLS AS FIRST-CLASS CITIZENS:
  
  - AITool: Specification (name, description, schema)
  - AIFunction: Implementation (executable code)
  - AIFunctionFactory: Creates AIFunction from .NET methods
  - Automatic discovery: Parameters/return types from reflection
  - JSON serialization: Automatic marshalling of parameters
  - Type safety: Compile-time method signatures

CONFIGURATION HIERARCHY:
  
  Agent-Level (ChatClientAgentOptions):
    - Applied to all runs
    - Default instructions, tools, history provider
  
  Run-Level (ChatClientAgentRunOptions):
    - Per-call overrides
    - Can inject per-request ChatClient decorator
    - ChatOptions override/merge
  
  Request-Level (ChatOptions):
    - Temperature, max tokens, model
    - Tools list (union with agent tools)
    - Instructions (supplement agent instructions)

TOOL CONTROL:
  
  ChatOptions.ToolMode controls LLM behavior:
    - Auto: Use tools when beneficial
    - None: Can't use tools
    - Required: Must use a tool
  
  Function approval workflows:
    - ToolApprovalAgent can require human review
    - Tool result can be intercepted/modified
    - Reject tool execution if needed

================================================================================
SECURITY CONSIDERATIONS (from docs)
================================================================================

1. HALLUCINATION
   - LLM may invent facts
   - Validate outputs from tool results

2. INDIRECT PROMPT INJECTION
   - Tool results might contain adversarial content
   - Sanitize data before passing to tools

3. MALICIOUS PAYLOADS
   - LLM output can contain harmful content
   - Sanitize before rendering HTML/executing

4. TOOL INVOCATION
   - By default, all tools invoked without approval
   - Consider ToolApprovalAgent for sensitive operations
   - Treat function arguments as untrusted input

RECOMMENDATIONS:
  - Validate and sanitize all LLM output
  - Apply defense-in-depth (multiple layers)
  - Require approval for sensitive tool operations
  - Use tool execution policies
  - Implement audit logging

================================================================================
""")

        <member name="M:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgentExtensions.AsAIFunction(Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent,Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunctionFactoryOptions,Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentSession)">
            <summary>
            Creates an <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunction"/> that runs the provided <see cref="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent"/>.
            </summary>
            <param name="agent">The <see cref="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AIAgent"/> to be represented as an invocable function.</param>
            <param name="options">
            Optional metadata to customize the function representation, such as name and description.
            If not provided, defaults will be inferred from the agent's properties.
            </param>
            <param name="session">
            Optional <see cref="T:Microsoft.Agents.AI.AgentSession"/> to use for function invocations. If not provided, a new session
            will be created for each function call, which may not preserve conversation context.
            </param>
            <returns>
            An <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunction"/> that can be used as a tool by other agents or AI models to invoke this agent.
            </returns>
            <exception cref="T:System.ArgumentNullException"><paramref name="agent"/> is <see langword="null"/>.</exception>
            <remarks>
            <para>
            This extension method enables agents to participate in function calling scenarios, where they can be
            invoked as tools by other agents or AI models. The resulting function accepts a query string as input and
            returns the agent's response as a string, making it compatible with standard function calling interfaces
            used by AI models.
            </para>
            <para>
            The resulting <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.AIFunction"/> is stateful, referencing both the <paramref name="agent"/> and the optional
--
            </summary>
        </member>
